figures.caption: Table 1: Marginal contributions for features in chain DAG example.

Figure 1: Overview of the MinShap algorithm and variations. a○: Shapley values are approximated by averaging marginal contributions/feature importance over K randomly sampled permutations; b○: MinShap only tests the minimum contribution under faithfulness in DAGs; c○: each feature importance test statistic yields a p-value test; d○: connect MinShap to Max-p via multiple test perspective; e○: PCHT, a resolution for conservativeness of Max-p.

Figure 4: Average performance measure with standard deviation of different number of permutation for linear and non-linear model with sample size 3000. We see that when Type II error is nearly 0, more permutations drive higher performance of MinShap and Max-p.
Table 3: Jaccard Index for the stability comparison of multiple testing based and stability selection based feature selection methods on model (a)–(d) using XGBoost.
Table 7: Comparison of methods in terms of prediction error and selection stability on diabetes data under two models.

Figure 3: Average performance measure and time with standard deviation for model (a)-(d) for multiple testing based and stability selection based feature selection comparison using XGBoost. We see that MinShap and Max-p are more efficient than stability-based feature selection methods.

Figure 10: Average performance measure with standard deviation for model (a)-(d) for feature selection comparison using Neural Network. We see that MinShap and Max-p outperform other feature selection methods and have well-controlled Type I error in the meanwhile.

Figure 2: Average performance measure with standard deviation for model (a)-(d) for feature selection comparison using XGBoost. We see that MinShap and Max-p outperform other feature selection methods.
Table 4: Comparison of methods in terms of prediction error and selection stability on wine quality data under two models.

Figure 9: Average performance with standard deviation of high-dimensional setting for linear and non-linear model. We see the advantage of the suite of adjusted p-value tests in in high-dimensional setting.

Figure 8: Average performance with standard deviation of different sample size for linear and non-linear model. We see the advantage of the suite of adjusted p-value (Bonferroni, Stouffer, Fisher) tests in lower-sample setting.

Figure 7: Average performance measure with standard deviation of fixed number of significant features with increasing number of total features for linear and non-linear model. We see that MinShap and Max-p work well under sparsity regime.
Table 5: Comparison of methods in terms of prediction error and selection stability on California housing data under two models.

Figure 5: Average performance measure with standard deviation of different number of permutation for linear and non-linear model with sample size 1000. We see that when Type II error is between 0 and 1, more permutations drive higher Type II error of MinShap and Max-p.
Figure 6: Average performance with standard deviation of varied proportion of significant features for linear and non-linear model. We see the advantage of the adjusted p-value tests in dense regime.
